desc:
There is a small slope here and the old street continues to the east and west
almost horizontally. To the west the street runs towards a tall watchtower made
out of stone, standing near the tall city wall. The buildings around you are
neatly and delicately built, the walls covered with colorful flowers in some
spots.
name: Valinor Street

desc:
The street is sloping east-west here. The old, ornamented buildings are
well-maintained but slightly stained by the salt sea winds from the gulf of
Lhun. Connected to the city wall to the south is a spiral staircase leading up
to a tower.
name: Valinor Street

desc:
This long and proud street is named after the Valar's domain which is now beyond
the circles of the earth but was reachable when the city was founded. It ends in
a flat, open place west of here and also leads east. A strong animal smell is
coming from the north.
name: Valinor Street

desc:
The Valinor Street ends here, not far from the Harlond quay. Only a few small
buildings without any ornaments stand among the trees here. The street continues
to the east, and to the west you can see the edge of the city quay with moored
boats of many different types. The faint sound of water lapping against stone
mixed with occasional shrieks from seagulls drifts in from the Gulf of Lhun.
name: End of Valinor Street

desc:
You are on a round part of the quay, paved with white, recently cleaned stones.
In the centre, a slender tree has been planted. Its white flowers illuminate the
surrounding facades without casting shadows anywhere. A long pier stretches out
to the west, and a proud and wide street begins to the east. The quay, lined
with boats of all sizes, begins to the south and continues further north.
name: Light Court

desc:
You are standing on a narrow jetty, no more than six feet across, which runs out
towards a tall, ancient lighthouse to the west. The rocks are a bit slippery so
you have to walk slowly. Boats lie on both sides and cold waves bounce them
slowly up and down.
name: Light Jetty

desc:
A cold, salt wind blows across the jetty here, and some waves are big enough to
wash your feet. A tall, stone-built lighthouse stands nearby, having withstood
thousands of storms. Its entrance, through a wooden door, is to the north, and
the way back is to the east.
name: Light Jetty

desc:
Built by the first elven settlers of Grey Havens in the beginning of the Second
Age, this lighthouse stands as upright as ever. Its walls are of solid stone
taken from the dwarven quarries of Ered Luin and they show little sign of
erosion. A spiral staircase leads up, and there is a wooden trapdoor which
presumably leads down.
name: Inside the Lighthouse

desc:
You have entered the main floor of a small tower. A large red banner hangs from
the north wall, on which a resplendent crest has been depicted. On the eastern
edge of the room, a large stairwell allows passage to the upper floor, while to
the south a staircase has been carved out of the rough rock, leading down. The
room is lit by sconces built into the walls, but is otherwise bare.
name: A Large Foyer

desc:
This is the main quay for ships coming from Pelargir, the port of Anduin and
Minas Tirith. A few ships still travel that route, and the quay is far from
empty despite the Shadow near Pelargir. You can walk north and south.
name: Pelargir Quay

desc:
Large ships recently arrived from the cities of Belfalas and Lebennin are docked
here. Some of them are in need of repair and you notice some Elves working on a
few of them. South of here ships from Pelargir lie and the quay continues to the
north. The Gulf of Lhun stretches out to the west, its salty water lapping
against the grey quay.
name: Grand Quay

desc:
Several large ships, both the slender, elven ships and the sturdier-looking
man-made vessels are moored here. The quay continues south and east.
name: Grand Quay

desc:
The great elven ships departing for Valinor leave from this pier. Many long
farewells have occurred at this pier. As each month passes, more and more elves
tire of Middle-earth and decide to sail back to Valinor in the west. You can go
back to the quay to the east. A wooden barrier prevents you from reaching the
western end of the pier.
name: The Pier of the Grey Havens

desc:
This is the large market-place where goods arriving by ship are auctioned out,
traded, and transported to distant kingdoms. Large quays go further north and
west.
name: Grand Market

desc:
This is where the large ships enter Harlond. The huge white sails are fluttering
in the salt breeze, and sailors are climbing around in the rigs. The quay
continues north, and connects to a market south. A pier reaches out into the
Gulf of Lhun to the west.
name: Grand Quay

desc:
The finest sails on this side of the ocean are made here. They are white as
snow, light and strong enough to take advantage of fearsome gales. The exit to
the quay is west.
name: Sailmaker's Workshop

desc:
The narrow street continues north-south between both small and large buildings,
most of which are very basically built and seem robust. There are almost no
shops here, and the buildings seem to be used as workshops and for storage. The
wind carries the smells of fish and salt water from the Gulf of Lhun that lies
to the north.
name: Old Ropemaker's Street

desc:
The strong smell of fish from the north blends into the rich atmosphere of a
crowded street filled with many workshops and restaurants and a faint
bitter-sweet scent of unknown origin from the west. An elaborate metal sign
representing a shiny sword entangled in seaweed hangs outside a small shop to
the east. The street goes north-south but is so narrow that a cat could easily
jump from one balcony to another.
name: Old Ropemaker's Street

desc:
The scent of a thousand rare herbs blend together in the air of this tiny but
well stocked shop. Hundreds of small pots and jars with mystic inscriptions fill
the shelves along with fresh herbs in water-filled vases and oddly-shaped
containers filled with spices with remote, exotic and sometimes unknown origins.
Your mind feels heavy, and you suspect that this is a subtle trick of the
shopkeeper to make you pay more.
name: Herbalist

desc:
From here, the fishing-boats go out before sunrise, and return many hours later,
with hopefully a good load of fish to sell on the market. The quay is littered
with wooden boxes, rope coils, buckets, and much else that the elven fishermen
didn't bother to stuff away. The market is east, and the quay continues west.
name: Fish Quay

desc:
This is where the ferry docks. Riding this can take you to Forlond on the other
side of the Gulf of Lhun. The quay continues east and south.
name: Ferry Quay

desc:
From far away, the great ships arrive here, carrying a precious load of herbs
and spices. A bitter-sweet scent almost makes you forget the intense smell of
fish, and there is a small shop, striking in its insignificance, to the east.
The quay continues north and south.
name: Herb Quay

desc:
This is the famous market in Grey Havens where all the delicacies from the sea
can be found at the high quality, needed to satisfy the demand of Elven
gourmets. Fishmongers are loudly advertising their loot and prices, and the
whole place smells not only of fish, but also of oysters, crabs, edible seaweed,
and everything else. There are quays to the north and west, a street going
south, and an inn to the east.
name: Fish Market

desc:
Ahh, what a lovely smell! Fishing-boats arrive with the gifts of the sea ready
to be sold on the market or brought home to the fishermen's wives. The market is
south and you can also go east.
name: Fish Quay

desc:
This is where the fishermen of the Havens hang up their nets to let them dry. It
makes the place look as if giant spiders lived here, hence the name. Old
fishermen sit on wooden benches, mending a few meshes in their favourite nets.
You can go east and west from here, and the Gulf of Lhun is directly north.
name: Spider Yard

desc:
The street is crowded, and voices speaking many languages are mixed with the
smells of newly caught fish from the market to the north, food from the nearby
restaurants, and wood, stone, ivory, leather, cloth, and hemp from the elven
craftsmen's workshops. The street goes north and south, and there is a shop of
some kind to the west.
name: Old Ropemaker's Street

desc:
You are standing in a narrow square surrounded by old buildings, a multitude of
shops, restaurants, bars, ateliers, and many happy elven citizens. A large well
dominates the scene. The well is always full of clear, fresh water and serves
not only as a water source but also as the meeting place in the inner parts of
Harlond. Passages lead off in all directions. In particular, the Seagull Inn is
located to the west.
name: Osse's Well

desc:
The narrow passage, named after the well in the inner town, goes north and south
here. At its narrowest place, you can touch the house walls on both sides of the
passage with your arms stretched out, but people pass each other elegantly
anyway.
name: Well Passage

desc:
Despite being a bit removed from the other shops in town, many customers are
moving about in here. Many different pets are available. You can hear a
multitude of different sounds in here. It really is a bit loud. If you would
like a pet, you should probably decide quickly. A grey-haired elven shopkeeper
is standing here, cleaning up after the animals.
name: Pet Shop

desc:
This is the most famous meeting-place in Harlond where people of all sorts
exchange news, rumours, deals and friendships. Sailors from the entire coast of
Middle-earth, as far as Dol Amroth and even Pelargir, are frequent guests here.
For the sleepy, there is a reception and chambers upstairs.
name: Seagull Inn

desc:
A vast variety of ropes are made here, from common tarred hemp-rope to soft
elven ropes, which are far stronger than their size appears. Also, the heavy,
coarse ropes used for ships' rigging are woven here.
name: Ropeworks

desc:
This spacious barn has obviously been set up to function as a stable. Along two
facing walls are arranged stalls and loose-boxes. At the end of each of the
stalls pegs and stout racks have been fashioned, to accommodate tack and feed
buckets; the uniformity of these suggests that perhaps these stalls are used by
the mounts of the Elven garrison stationed here. The loose-boxes, by contrast,
appear to be used less regularly, and are most likely for livery animals. But
whether for steeds of citizens or visitors, the accommodations are universally
clean and well-maintained, with thick beds of new straw on the floor, and clear
water in long troughs for every animal.
name: Harlond's Stable

desc:
Happy to find a bed that does not heave and roll, sailors and travellers can get
a good night's sleep here with their belongings safely stashed away. On the way
to your room you see a bulletin board. It appears that you can easily find out
about changes in Middle-earth. The Inn is downstairs.
name: Seagull Reception

desc:
Grey Havens is a peaceful town and not famous for its fine weapons, but the good
old elven craftmanship guarantees that no junk is sold here. Few of these
weapons ever find other places than as decorations in chic homes, but if need
be, they are as sharp and deadly as any. The only exit from the small shop is to
the west.
name: Faldor's Fine Weapons

desc:
Although the lane is narrow, there is still room for trees planted at irregular
intervals. They help to give shadow during sunny days. The people filling the
streets have lived here far longer than mortals imagine and have had plenty of
time to create a pleasant atmosphere. To the west you can catch a glimpse of
Osse's well and the lane continues to the north.
name: Crab Lane

desc:
The street turns south-east here, but an open space lies north through a small
arch. The surrounding houses are old, some plain, some ornamented but all
decorated with red and yellow flowers and an occasional climbing plant.
name: Crab Lane

desc:
This is a small yard that happened to be left open by the surrounding houses.
Elven kids are playing here, and the local cats are hunting for mice in the
corners. A low archway leads out to the street to the south.
name: Back Yard

desc:
The smell of fish, fowl, tar and old tobacco greets you as you enter the old
inn. Fishermen are sitting at wooden tables discussing the movements of the
shoals, how to hunt sharks the best way, why the prices keep falling on the
market, and telling some rather tall fish tales. The terrific atmosphere here is
partly produced by the numerous fishing items hung on the walls including
anchors, nets and stuffed fish, and partly by the great smelling dishes served
here. You also see some duck-calling instruments attached to the wall. Many
people here are eating from large plates of food. The exit is west, and there is
another room to the south.
name: The Spicy Swordfish House

desc:
This room is richly decorated by paintings and many old artefacts from the sea
and it is enjoyed by sailors and townspeople alike. Many important decisions are
informally made here while dining on one of the inn's specialities and a glass
of wine. A number of people are sitting at tables here enjoying their meal. The
only exit is north.
name: Inner Room

desc:
This tower rises about ten feet above the city wall and gives a commanding view
of the surrounding area. You have a commanding view of the mountains of Ered
Luin to the south. In times of war this tower would enable the elves to know
well in advance if there was an approaching army from the south. The only exit
is down.
name: Watchtower

desc:
The ceiling of this room has been ingeniously constructed of glass panes,
allowing an unobstructed view of the heavens, the sprawling city of Grey Havens,
and the magnificent ocean to the west. A large tubular device is pointed out an
opening in the windows towards the sky. Several comfortable chairs and large
tables have been set out, and you can see a cluttered laboratory partially
concealed by drapes against the north wall. One can see a large wine rack and
beside it a finely crafted cupboard. In the centre of the room, a low marble
fence surrounds an earthen platform, from which grows a sturdy young tree, no
older than twenty years. Its broad green leaves rustle pleasantly in the sea
breezes.
name: A Cluttered Observatory

desc:
The impressive height of the lighthouse gives a splendid view over Harlond, with
its mighty ships and crowded narrow streets, as well as Forlond on the other
side of the gulf of Lhun. Far away, the blue hills of Ered Luin can be seen, but
even the sharp eyes of the elves cannot see anything but the grey sea to the
west anymore. Securely held by slender bronze arms, a crystal orb of rare beauty
and luminance provides light that is seen from far out at sea. A spiral
staircase descends downward.
name: Top of Lighthouse

desc:
This is the famous bar under the lighthouse, buried under the ancient
construction. The place is dry (with the possible exception of its clientele),
in spite of its location under sea level, and comfortably furnitured with wooden
tables and an impressive marble bar.
name: Lighthouse Pub

desc:
Here the pier stands clear of the land, jutting far out into the Gulf of Lhun,
so that the mighty elven sailing ships might safely dock. Constructed from huge
blocks of pale stone, the jetty barely shows any sign of the passing years and
relentless punishment by the elements. Sturdy metal loops are mounted into the
stone at regular intervals, for mooring ropes, but otherwise this construction
is quite unadorned.
name: At the End of the Pier

desc:
This is the midships and the lowest of the three exposed decks. The great
mainmast of white timber dominates the centre of the vessel, secured to the
sides of the ship by shrouds of silvery rope. Otherwise, this sailing vessel is
surprisingly free of clutter - everything has its place, and its place is not to
interfere with passage upon the decks. The deck is of a pale grey wood and has a
slight camber to runoff the water that comes aboard. Pale golden rigging
provides access to the square rigged mainsail of white canvas, which is hung
from the main yard. Shining from the very heights of the mast is a lamp which
casts a pale brilliance upon the deck. Before the mast, steps lead up to the
foredeck and the magnificent prow, while steps abaft lead to the aft-deck and
the wheel to steer the vessel by. On either side, silver brows provide access to
the shore when docked.
name: Upon the White Ship

desc:
This small but robust vessel is actually a real sailing boat, since sailing
across the Lhun might be treacherous at times. The elven boat is made out of
pale timbers with some small ornaments. The sails are furled and secure, while
the ferry is docked to the quay of Forlond.
name: Aboard the Ferry (at the quay in Forlond)

desc:
You have reached the end of the quay. The ferry which travels across the Gulf of
Lhun to Harlond departs from here. You see a note here attached to a post which
gives more details. The quay extends back to the west and there is a small beach
to the north.
name: Ferry Dock

desc:
The dock here is filled with many small ships and boats. Most of them seem to be
ships used for pleasure trips. One ship has just left the dock apparently
heading off for a day of sailing. The quay continues to the east and west and
there is a shop to the north.
name: Small Ship Dock

desc:
The smell in the air is enough to tell you that this is the fish quay. Many
elven fishing boats are here while many others can be seen out in the Gulf of
Lhun. Along the quay are many scraps of fish dropped by the fisherman who are
not always the cleanest people.
name: Fish Quay

desc:
This is part of the fish quay. A number of fishermen are here unloading the fish
they have caught. In the large fishing nets you can see hundreds of mackerel and
cod. It appears to have been a very good fishing day. The fish quay extends
north and you can probably slip on board their fishing boat to the south.
name: Fish Quay

desc:
You are standing on a large fishing boat. Many elves are running back and forth
here almost seeming not to see you. Some are at work cleaning the boat while
others are inspecting the mast and the rigging. The boat is quite large. There
are four masts reaching up into the sky. The sails attached to them flutter
rapidly in the wind here. You can also see many large fishing nets hanging here
drying.
name: Aboard a Large Fishing Boat

desc:
This quay is reserved for ships trading in textiles. At times there are ships
here coming all the way from Pelargir. At least you are now away from the
overwhelming fish smell. You can hardly imagine how the elves get used to it.
The quay extends east and west.
name: Textile Quay

desc:
This is where many of the large ships enter Forlond. Many ships are currently
docked here. It is interesting to compare the elven ships with the ones here
obviously made by man. The man-made ships look sturdier but somehow you doubt
that they are. In any case it is very windy here and the sails flutter rapidly
back and forth. Many sailors are hard at work on their ships either making
simple repairs or helping to load or unload their goods. The shipyard lies to
the south while the quay continues east.
name: Grand Quay

desc:
This is the part of Forlond's shipyard devoted to repairing damaged ships. The
winds upon the Gulf of Lhun are quite strong and the waves they produce often
wreak havoc on the great elven ships. Some of the ships need minor repairs like
needing new sails, but others have more severe damage like destroyed masts or
even actual hull damage. The elves working here are very good and in a short
time these ships will be seaworthy again.
name: Shipyard

desc:
This is half of the shipyard for Forlond. This area seems to be devoted to
building new ships. There are several half-built ships here as well as some
quite close to completion. One in particular is receiving its last touches of
paint and will soon be ready to ride upon the sea. Despite all the hard work,
the elves here are singing merry songs most likely written by shipbuilders
millennia ago.
name: Shipyard

desc:
This open area has all the supplies any fisherman needs. You can see many
fishing poles and nets. There are also many sails hanging here wavering in the
wind. Having them outside like this is certainly the best way for the customers
to see the sails' quality. There are also many piles of rope used for a ship's
rigging and wood that can be used for a ship's hull or masts. The shipyard to
the south probably gets many of their supplies here. Hung on an old coil of rope
is a small sign.
name: Fishermen's Supply Shop

desc:
This is a short wooden pier reaching out into the Gulf of Lhun. At times many
elven fisherman can be found sitting here casting away all morning and
afternoon. A narrow jetty lies further to the south and several half-built ships
can be seen to the east.
name: Short Pier

desc:
This short jetty leads toward a tall lighthouse. About ten feet in width and
forty feet long, the jetty has been constructed from the natural rocks of the
harbour. As the waves of the Gulf of Lhun splash upon them, the rocks are wet
and slippery underfoot, and this place seems to be permanently assailed by
strong gusts of wind. Nevertheless it seems a pleasant place.
name: Windblown Jetty

desc:
This ancient lighthouse, and the one which stands in Harlond, across the Gulf of
Lhun, were built to guide ships into the docks of the Grey Havens. Its stone
walls seem untouched by the passage of time, as does the circular iron stair-
case, which leads upward, its way lit by a row of torches fastened to the wall.
name: Lighthouse

desc:
This very large building houses most of the town's guards. It is sectioned off
into many small areas so each guard has some private space. There is also a
common dining area with an enormous oak table. Several guards are here at this
time. They appear to be telling each other some amusing stories but you aren't
sure what about. You can exit back east or there is a another door to the west.
name: Garrison

desc:
This relatively large room is where the garrison commander lives. He has a bed
more than large enough for his tall frame. A window on the southern wall gives
him a wonderful view of the Gulf and the lighthouse. It is obvious that he is a
master of commanding forces. The walls are covered with weapons such as bows and
longswords. There are also several intricately designed shields used as
decorations. Small braziers high up on the walls light the room. Your only exit
is back east.
name: Commander's Office

desc:
During the night this is where many of the fishing nets are stored. They are
hung here so they can be dry by morning. A number of piles of fish scraps are
lying here indicating that this area is also used for cleaning and skinning the
fish they have caught. A number of fishermen are here working.
name: Fishing Yard

desc:
Though this lighthouse is not as high as the one in Harlond, it still provides a
wonderful view of the area. The city of Forlond stretches out before you and the
background is dominated by the many peaks of Ered Luin. Across the Gulf of Lhun
you can see the many lights of Harlond. In the centre of the room stands a solid
marble pedestal, atop which rests the brilliant orb.
name: Top of Lighthouse

desc:
The access to the decks below, the aftdeck provides sail by means of a lateen
rig attached to the mizzenmast. Lanyards of silvery twine attach to the bottom
of the loose-footed mizzen when unfurled. At the stern stands the elf at the
helm, holding the elegantly carved wheel which turns the black rudder below. To
port and starboard are lamps which constantly illuminate the aft.
name: The Aftdeck

desc:
Centred upon the forward deck is the large foremast, overshadowed only by the
mainmast. Square rigged, the yards hold the two curved foresails of the mast.
Though impressive, the most striking feature of the foredeck is the prow, carved
in the curving shape of a swan's neck of snowiest white. Its beak shines in the
light like burnished gold, and its eyes are golden sapphires with pupils of jet.
So it is, that the white ship looks as a vast swan when seen approaching from
afar. Housed on the portside is the large adamantine chain which secures the
anchor to the deck of the ship whilst silvery breast ropes from behind the prow
provide secure moorings when berthed.
name: The Foredeck

desc:
You have entered a vast chamber with rough and irregular stone walls which rise
almost fifty feet above you. The floor has been carved smooth, and against the
northern end an area has been furnished with carpets, a large desk, several
shelves and a huge canopied bed. The area is poorly lit by several sconces built
into the walls, barely revealing the corners of the room. The air is cool and
moist, and you can hear the trinkle of water echoing faintly.
name: A Vaulted Chamber of Stone

desc:
Several decks are built below, each open from fore to aft and smelling of spices
and fragrant foods. Partitions of embroidered cloth provide privacy to those who
want it, but generally the passengers prefer to experience the sea breezes
above. Dunnage provides good stowage for cargo during carriage, but otherwise,
the only major sights are the piercing timber shafts of the masts above.
name: Below Decks

desc:
This beautiful garden is filled with colourful flowers carefully tended by one
of the finest gardeners on Arda. The air carries a rich sweet smell and
transports the soft fragrance of the blooms to the adjoining homes. Often times
some elves can be found here singing enchanting songs that may add warmth to the
hearts of those who listen. To the east of this garden lies the sanctuary of one
of Middle-earth's noble elves.
name: Musical Garden

desc:
Most of this structure's space is taken up by large wine barrels and racks upon
racks of bottles of elven brews ageing to perfection. In the southern corner
there is a place for the farmers employed by Sihir the Elf to work on mixing and
developing the various vintages. The northern corner seems to be used for the
bottling process.
name: Sihir's Winery

desc:
This balcony stands above a beautiful garden on a huge old oak tree. This place
is being used by the elves to chant their songs while watching the beautiful
garden below or the vineyard to the north. This place is also used by the owner
of the winery and his friends while enjoying the best weed of the Longbottoms.
name: Tree Balcony

desc:
This wide street named after the Gulf of Lhun to the south is made of small
sandstone blocks. Traffic on the street is quite high as many elves are out
shopping. The quay lies to the south while the street heads off to the north.
The city garrison lies to the west.
name: Lhun Street

desc:
Though large, and apparently quite often frequented by the horses belonging to
the elven guardsmen, this paved yard is kept completely clean. To the south a
large wooden building dominates the area, and to the west a broad gateway opens
onto Lhun Street beyond.
name: In a Well-kept Stable Yard

desc:
Rows of spacious loose-boxes line the back wall of the building, with fresh
straw covering their floors, and a stout trough of fresh water at the rear of
each one. At one end of the building stand massive bins for oats and bran, and
nest to these are piles many buckets. Above them neat rows of pegs are affixed
to the wall, where halters and ropes might be hung. A small sign, penned in a
meticulous hand, is pinned next to the pegs.
name: The Stables

desc:
This simple circular room has been built of white stone. Although the stone work
looks ancient everything is in good shape. There is a wide firepit in the centre
of the room, offering warmth and cooking facilities. The other furniture
consists of a small bed, a wooden table, some chairs and cabinets. Two big round
windows offer a view to a small square to the north. A tranquil atmosphere
prevails in the room.
name: Faiozf's Enclave

desc:
This is a quiet, serene garden. Luscious plants, beautifuly flowers, and
sculpted shrubbery grow abundantly in a welcoming landscape, providing a brief
respite from the hustle of life. A few marked paths lead deeper into the flora,
allowing a more intimate encounter with nature.
name: Limp's Serene Garden

desc:
Sparkling and shining in the light from the stars and the sun, a babbling stream
emerges from the ground here and starts the long journey through the vineyard
towards the River Lhun. Beyond the spring, a stone paths ascends to an upper
section that provides a spectacular view.
name: Sparkling Spring
